posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Chateau Climens is my second favorite Sauternes/Barsac. Pretty far ahead of anything but Yquem, and, esp. considering the price differences, not that far behind it. Of recent vintages, I must admit that while I have purchased some 2015 and 2016, I have not opened any yet.

My favorite Climens is the 2001. Just a brilliant wine. I also love the 1986. I have had this multiple times including this past week, and it is fabulous. My third favorite maybe a bit more of a long-shot than the first two (which are pretty well known to be great) but I just love the 2014 the couple of times I have had it. I will be interested in how it will age but I am not sure I will find out as I am not sure I can keep my hands off of my remaining bottles.

posts.name: Ed Steinway
posts.cooked:
2001 and 1990 are my favorite vintages for Climens. I have only had the 2014 a few times but I agree with you, Howard. It is excellent. The 2005 is also very good.

Ed

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: 09 is very good

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked: My favorites are '90, '78, and '88. Followed closely by '86, '71, and '07. I’ve only had the '01 twice and never in its youth when I hear it was incredible, but I don’t think it’s as strong as it should be. (One other at the tasting disagreed with me.)

posts.name: Jay $$ Winton
posts.cooked: I drank a 375 of 07 last year-far too young but delicious then and only will improve. 88 is excellent now.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Can’t disagree with all the above. I will say that 2003 is remarkably good, in its way. Maybe not a favorite, but deserves mention for the quality in such a difficult vintage.

posts.name: suzanne_camhi
posts.cooked: love the 88 Climens

posts.name: Mark Ye
posts.cooked: Loved 75 and 01 personally.

posts.name: Ken Strauss
posts.cooked:
Agree about 1988.
Exceeded expectations.

posts.name: David Lewin
posts.cooked: I too love the 1988. Five years ago, I served it and the 1989 together and while I loved the opulence of the 1989, the structure and balance of the 1988 stood out. I will drink my last bottle of 1989 in about a month, but see no hurry to open the 1988. When I started out collecting wine in the 70’s, I had a wonderful bottle of 1949 which I remember after all these years, unlike most other wines I tasted 40 years ago.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
1988, 2001, 1986

+1. The one time I had ‘75, it was spectacular. I like ‘07 too.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
I’ve loved both ‘86 and ‘88, must pick up some of these younger vintages to try.

It’s curious that 1991-2000 are so poorly regarded. A decade of futility? Any ideas what was going wrong at Climens in the 90s? I know the 90s had only a few very good vintages in Bordeaux, but Sauternes tends to dance to its own drummer. Was this a Climens problem or reflect bigger overall regional weakness?

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked:
I’ve loved both ‘86 and ‘88, must pick up some of these younger vintages to try.

It’s curious that 1991-2000 are so poorly regarded. A decade of futility? Any ideas what was going wrong at Climens in the 90s? I know the 90s had only a few very good vintages in Bordeaux, but Sauternes tends to dance to its own drummer. Was this a Climens problem or reflect bigger overall regional weakness?

It was a weak decade for Sauternes overall. I don’t think any of those vintages of Climens will ever compare with something like '88, but it’s also priced much cheaper. I had a '95 Climens recently which I bought for $60-70, and it was superb. I would definitely buy '95, '96, '97, and to a lesser extent '98 and '99 if the pricing was right. The same is true for Rieussec, Yquem, etc (but avoid the '99 Yquem…).

posts.name: Jeff King
posts.cooked: I went long on the 2001 and certainly haven’t regretted it so far. That said, I doubt it will quite measure up to the '71, maybe the '75. But tough to compare something in your glass to memories or old tasting notes.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: 1988 of vintages I have had, all post 1987.
